title.text: Understanding and improving psychological, social and physical quality-of-life outcomes for paediatric burn patients an

description.text:
Quality of life is dependent on psychological and physical wellbeing. Paediatric burn patients have an increased long-term risk of a variety of serious health conditions such as cardiovascular disease, diabetes, infectious disease, mental health conditions and cancer (1-6). Chronic inflammation and sustained changes to the immune system after a burn may be related to the increase in morbidities seen in this population regardless of burn size and scar outcome (7, 8).
Psychologically, poor mental health outcomes are more likely in a child who has been hospitalised for a burn injury compared to a peer who has not had a burn, and this remains true regardless of burn size, severity, and scar outcome. Psychosocial screening of patients and parents postburn might help us to understand baseline indicators of poor longer-term mental health recovery.
Physically, adults who have had a burn injury participate less in leisure-related physical activity (9), and have lower physical fitness (10). Paediatric burn patients might also have lower physical activity levels and impaired fitness resulting from physical and physiological limitations imposed by the burn, psychological limitations of the patient, overprotectiveness of the parent, or due to comorbidities secondary to burn.

title.text: A retrospective analysis of leisure and lifestyle data across rural and metropolitan Mission Australia Aged Care Services

description.text: The average age of residents within Australian aged care facilities is 86 years old, comprising of 50% men and 50% women. This research project is focused on the aged care residents of the three Mission Australia aged care facilities, of which service a unique population. The people Mission Australia supports experience significant disadvantages such as physical or mental health concerns, and do not have a safe place to call home. As such, the age range in these sites is much younger, from 50-90 years old, and the gender breakdown is more male dominated, with 75% males and 25% females. Therefore, standardized aged care leisure and lifestyle applications may not be as relevant to these sites and this survey, which the government requires to be undertaken, will allow tailored consumer-centred care. The aim of our project is to analyse and compare the offerings leisure and lifestyle of rural and metropolitan Mission Australia Aged Care facilities and to facilitate more meaningful activities. The study objective is to ensure the leisure and lifestyle services provided to consumers at Mission Australia Aged Care sites are appropriate and relatable to them, as well as find aspects that need improvement.

title.text: Voices of school leaders: Exploring exemplary leadership practices that optimise student engagement and learning in an AI world in NSW secondary schools

description.text: The primary aim of this research project is to identify and analyse the exemplary strategies employed by NSW school leaders to optimise student motivation and engagement in learning within an AI-integrated educational environment. The central research question is: "What are the exemplary strategies that NSW school leaders employ to optimise student motivation and engagement in learning in an AI world?" This is supported by six sub-questions exploring the impact of AI on engagement, specific strategies implemented by leaders, the rationale behind those strategies, success factors in AI integration, the role of inclusive practices, and enhanced student learning outcomes resulting from AI use.

title.text: Herpes zoster vaccination and new diagnoses of dementia: A quasi-randomized study in the Lumos healthcare database

description.text: This project examines whether herpes zoster (HZ) vaccination reduces the risk of new dementia diagnoses. Using Lumos data, we aim to replicate earlier findings from the PenCS dataset, comparing outcomes in those aged 70–79 (eligible for vaccination in 2016) with those aged 80 and over (not eligible). With no cure for dementia and rising case numbers, identifying modifiable risk factors is critical. This analysis offers quasi-experimental evidence that may inform immunisation policy and support broader efforts to reduce the burden of dementia through prevention.

title.text: Evaluating the acceptability of an Acupuncture Program in an Inpatient Antenatal Hospital Unit: A Pilot Study

description.text:
There is a growing global demand to approach maternal healthcare in a more woman-centred, culturally sensitive, and humanised way (16). A national survey from Australia indicates that around half of all women giving birth experience some level of birth trauma within the maternity system, and that 85% of women would make different choices for their next birth (17). A humanised approach to maternal health care not only addresses the physical needs of mothers but also prioritises their mental, emotional, and social well-being. The evidence demonstrates that such approaches lead to better health outcomes, higher satisfaction, and reduced disparities, making it a beneficial model for maternal health care (18,19, 20, 21). Acupuncture supports humanised holistic care by providing personalised, integrative, and culturally sensitive treatments that address the mind-body connection, and thus, can play a supportive role in maternity care (22). Acupuncture’s ability to reduce stress, improve mental health (23,24, 25) and manage pain (26, 27) aligns well with the principles of holistic and humanised care and highlights acupuncture's role in enhancing patient-centred, comprehensive health care (28). 
Acupuncture services are not new to BC Women's Hospital, as acupuncture has been offered at the ‘Families In Recovery’ (FIR) Unit for 2.5 years, and is the most requested and utilised allied health service on the unit. Verbal feedback from the FIR unit's patients suggest that acupuncture significantly improved conditions such as mental health, sleep quality, stress levels, and other concerns. However, no formal evaluation of the service offered at FIR has occurred. Because of the noted success in the FIR unit, a pilot
program to expand to the antenatal unit (Evergreen) and the Reproductive Mental Health outpatient unit was created. This study, which evaluates this service, will be conducted at BC Women's antenatal unit, Evergreen, to examine what conditions acupuncture is most commonly requested for, and to examine its benefits or harms. The findings will help determine whether acupuncture positively supports clinical practice and if the services should be continued or expanded.

title.text: The impact of 1:1 devices on student learning and engagement

description.text:
This research aims to examine the role of technology in teaching practice and the resulting impact of (technology-dominant) practice and 1:1 devices on student learning and engagement in SCS schools.

Objective
To investigate the advantages and challenges of using 1:1 devices compared to traditional notebook methods.
To gauge student engagement by measuring cognitive and affective engagement factors.

title.text: A phenomenological exploration of midwives’ use and perception of gender-neutral language in maternity settings.

description.text: This proposed research is rooted in a feminist phenomenological approach, offering a nuanced understanding of midwives' experiences with gender-neutral language. By prioritising midwives' voices and narratives via semi structured interviews, it proposes to uncover the hidden power dynamics and normative influences. This proposed study aligns with feminist principles and the pursuit of true gender equality, shedding light on the complexities surrounding gender-neutral language. This research offers a unique opportunity to understand midwives' perspectives on using gender-neutral language, inform policies and provide guidance to promote inclusivity. Using a phenomenological approach with semi-structured interviews aims to foster a more participatory and respectful engagement with midwife participants, reinforcing trust and candid sharing of experiences.

title.text: The Clinical Outcomes Of Indwelling Tunneled Pleural Catheter Use For Malignant Pleural Effusion In Metropolitan Sydney Hospitals

description.text: The purpose of this research project is to assess the clinical outcomes of patients using intercostal chest drain as a management of malignant pleural effusion within St George Hospital and Sutherland Hospital. It is a retrospective study assessing the clinical outcomes of managing malignant pleural effusion wit intercostal chest drain at St George Hospital and Sutherland Hospital, from 2019 to 2021.

title.text: The Aboriginal Land Inquiry: Recovering the Voices of the WA Land Rights Movement, 1983-1984

description.text:
This Honours project in History will investigate publicly available archival records held at the WA State Records Office about the 1983-1984 Western Australia Aboriginal Land Inquiry. In 1983, the Burke government commissioned the inquiry in an attempt to fulfill their election promise on land rights, appointing Justice Paul Seaman to find the best form of land rights for Western Australia. The inquiry emerged at a time of growing unrest and land rights activism among Aboriginal people, particularly in WA with the steady encroach of mining in the north-west, and creation of the Aboriginal Land Rights (Northern Territory) Act of 1976/77, that was passed by the Liberal Federal Government. Several hundred submissions were received from many sectors of WA society and more broadly.  These included Aboriginal organisations, the resource industry, pastoralists and graziers, community groups, academics, and lawyers. 
To date, limited to no historical research has been conducted on this project. This project seeks to place this Aboriginal Land Inquiry in its full historical context, including the fact that land – who owns it, and who has access to it – is at the core of settler-colonialism. This inquiry was the first time Indigenous communities had been asked about their vision for the future. This project seeks to recover their forgotten voices.

title.text: What effect does virtual reality simulation have on final-year nursing students’ competence and confidence in performing non-technical skills?

description.text: The PhD by publication will entail a three-phase approach to explore the research question by engaging with the clinical experts in the nursing field, measuring Non-technical skills (NTS) performance and confidence across two different simulation modalities (in situ/face to face and virtual reality) and to provide an intervention to ascertain if virtual reality (VR) specific NTS training can enhance NTS performance and confidence in final-year nursing students.

title.text: Bridging the cancer evidence gap to enhance prevention and early intervention and to reduce premature mortality among people experiencing homelessness

description.text: This project will extend the broader program of homeless health research undertaken by our research team (Home2Health) within the Institute for Health Research at UNDA. It will involve examining cancer screening, diagnosis, treatment, and health outcomes (including mortality) amongst a large cohort of people with lived experience of homelessness in WA, with the aim of addressing cancer-related evidence/data gaps for this population to improve opportunities for prevention, earlier intervention, and reduction in the frequency of premature cancer-related mortality in this priority population group. The research project is the subject of the recently awarded public health priority grant co-funded by the Future Health Research & Innovation Fund and Cancer Council WA.

title.text: Monitoring the Western Australian preterm birth prevention initiative

description.text: Preterm birth, defined as delivery before 37 weeks' gestation, poses significant risks to neonatal health and can lead to long-term consequences. Cervical shortening is a common precursor to preterm birth, and prevents the mechanical reinforcement of the cervix, in maintaining the pregnancy. Topical vaginal progesterone and cervical cerclage are medical interventions aimed at prolonging gestation and reducing preterm birth rates. While cerclage is more effective in extreme cases of shortening (<10mm), combining progesterone with cerclage shows promise in moderately shortened cervixes (25-10mm). This prospective pilot study seeks to assess the efficacy of this combined approach in increasing gestational age and minimising neonatal complications.

title.text: West Kimberley Climate Adaptation
Towards an Understanding of Research Capacity of Allied Health and Nursing and Midwifery Clinicians in the Kimberley Region of Western Australia

description.text: The aim of this project is to understand the research capacity of Allied Health (AH), and Nursing and Midwifery (NM) clinicians practicing in the Kimberley region of Western Australia.

title.text: Women's experiences of maternity care in Western Sydney Local Health District (WSLHD) a culturally and linguistically diverse (CALD) perspective

description.text:
In Australia more than 97% of women give birth in hospital. Western Sydney Local Health District (WSLHD) accounts for the highest proportion of births in NSW, and the highest proportion of women from culturally and linguistically diverse (CALD) backgrounds.[1] Women who are marginalized, including refugees, migrants, asylum seekers (collectively referred to as CALD), face a multitude of barriers to maternity services, [2] substantially impacting their care. The aim of the study is to understand what constitutes as barriers and facilitators in providing the optimum care for these women, and how we can use this information to facilitate respectful models of care
for them. We will be employing a qualitative study method ( with an interpreter), in-depth face to face individual interviews, utilizing theory - informed interpretive frameworks to understand the CALD's women's experiences.

title.text: Comparison of EchoSolv-AS with Human Diagnosis of Severe Aortic Stenosis from Echocardiographic Measurement Data

description.text:
Aortic Stenosis (AS) is a serious heart condition characterized by the narrowing of the aortic valve and is the cause of substantial morbidity and mortality in Australia. When both moderate and severe, AS is associated with an increased risk of death. The aim of this study is to examine the performance of AI in identifying severe AS compared with specialist Cardiologists, and whether the assistance of the AI (as the “gold standard”) has the potential to improve the quality and consistency of AS reporting.
The objective is to evaluate whether the EchoSolv-AS AI can improve the quality and consistency of AS reporting by correctly identifying guideline-severe AS and the severe AS phenotype.

title.text: Evaluating the implementation of the Spiritual Capabilities Framework for early years educators

description.text: This project seeks to explore the pedagogical implementation of the Spiritual Capabilities Framework (SCF) as experienced by early years educators. Since 2022, the researchers have worked with MercyCare to provide professional learning to educators on spirituality for young children. A SCF was developed that built on the researchers’ prior work along with the work undertaken with MercyCare. The SCF aims to provide a resource for professionals working with young children seeking to intentionally promote young children’s spirituality as part of their holistic development. This is the first Framework of its kind and as such, understanding the way educators make use of the Framework, and how they come to understand and promote children’s spirituality from its adoption, will be innovative, providing new knowledge in this field. The findings will be impactful for MercyCare specifically, but for the early years sector more broadly as they seek to invest in programs and approaches that assist educators in delivering quality early learning experiences.

title.text: The UNFOLD study - Investigating immunotherapy for chronic lung disease

description.text: The highly variable clinical outcomes have made it difficult to identify the precise mechanisms involved in the pathogenesis of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) and thus the development of a specific cure or treatment to halt and reverse the inexorable decline in patient health. Chronic inflammation is a cardinal feature of IPF and is driven by both innate and adaptive immune responses. We recently demonstrated a potential role for antibody secreting plasma B cells (PCs) in the development of bleomycin-induced lung fibrosis in mice. Human studies also showed both elevated serum autoantibodies and elevated numbers of antibody secreting cells in IPF blood, and the accumulation of mature PCs adjacent to areas of active lung fibrosis in IPF lung tissue, supporting a functional role for PCs in IPF pathogenesis. We have used a multi-omics approach to examine the B cell repertoire of a subset of IPF patients and age-matched controls as well as screening IPF patient sera against a proteomic array to identify potential human autoantigens. We have shown that the B cell repertoire in IPF differs to that of control patients, with IPF patients displaying a restricted antibody repertoire compared to controls and evidence of B cell clonal expansion. We have identified a range of novel autoantigens in a subset of IPF patients. These findings suggest an approach for stratifying IPF patients based on an autoimmune phenotype that may inform treatment options for some of these patients. The UNFOLD study investigates the potential of immunotherapy for this chronic lung disease and specifically will 1) Validate specific common autoantigens using larger, geographically disparate IPF patient cohorts. 2) develop more appropriate and sensitive screening assays for early diagnosis and stratification to identify autoimmune IPF patients and 3). Test the suitability of novel plasma cell-specific B cell ablation and CAR-T cell therapy approaches in pre-clinical models of lung fibrosis.

title.text: An Increase of Prazosin Association with Deliberate Self Harm Australia: a Retrospective Case Series

description.text: To conduct a retrospective observational study to assess the relationship between increased prescribing for off-label use of prazosin in mental health disorders (i.e. Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder [PTSD]) and medication related harm (i.e. medication therapeutic error, deliberate self-poisoning [DSP]). The population will include any person in Australia who have had the Poisons Information Centre NSW involved in their clinical management. The intervention group will be those who have been prescribed prazosin with an outcome of deliberately self-poisoning (DSP) and the changes in rates of this over time which will be held in comparison to overall rates of DSP cases in the Poisons Information Centre Database

title.text: Representations of filicide-suicide in the news: Feminist perspectives

description.text:
This research examines how filicide-suicide is represented in the Australian news. It will contribute to feminist perspectives on women's violence, and is intended for publication as a book chapter with International Handbook of Feminist Perspectives on Women’s Acts of Violence (Stacey Banwell, Ed.)
STATUS (11/04/2022): Draft Manuscript Submitted for Review

title.text: Social suffering and midwife mothers

description.text: This study seeks to explore the lived experience of Australian midwives who are mothers (MidMums) and to consider whether elements of social suffering contribute to their choice to remain or to leave the profession. Utilising a critical phenomenological approach, the lived experience of MidMums will be explored during interviews and then critically analysed with respect to the theoretical concept that is social suffering. Study findings have the potential to inform policies and procedures to improve midwives’ professional productivity and emotional wellbeing, and to have a broader impact upon society by contributing to improvements in conditions influencing female workforce, parental support, and gender pay equality.

title.text: Return to performance after a calf muscle strain injury in elite male Australian Football League players: A case-series

description.text: This project will evaluate time to return to performance in elite Australian football players following calf muscle injury.

title.text: Understanding the morbidity associated with treating Acute Myeloid Leukaemia (AML) in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children

description.text:
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children are less likely to be diagnosed with childhood cancers, but are also less likely to survive. By understanding how they tolerate treatment, their side effects experienced and long-term outcomes, we can modify future treatments and better manage their side effects for better survival/quality of life. Conduct a retrospective cohort study on Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children with Acute Myeloid Leukaemia, diagnosed at PMH/PCH between 2000 and 2020. Factors including sex, age, location of residence, survival, short- and long-term side effects will be examined. 

Project is a small proportion of a larger project with all childhood cancers being reviewed from the same timeframe mentioned above. Focusing on AML is for practicality for the capstone project as well as offering the larger project a focus on specifical treatments/side effects in AML experienced by the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children.

title.text: Self-enhancement, "Biohacking" and Spiritual Yearning

description.text: A qualitative investigation into the presence and nature of spiritual yearning among people engaging substantially in biohacking practices and unaffiliated with traditional religion. We will be interviewing 40 participants as part of this study.

title.text: Clinical and Patient-Reported Outcome Measures for Custom Implants used in Reverse Shoulder Arthroplasty
Evaluation to fuel advocacy for writer visits in Australian schools

description.text:
This project will explore how Australian schools evaluate and communicate the impact of writer visits. Based
on findings and Margaret’s expertise in evaluation
and literacy engagement, an open source tool will be developed to support schools’ effectiveness at capturing and promoting the impact of Australian writers on students’ reading engagement.

title.text: Doctors’ health and wellbeing research priorities in Australia and New Zealand: Consensus from a Delphi study.

description.text:
There is no current evidence-based information to guide what the research priority areas are for Doctors’ health and wellbeing in Australia and New Zealand. 

A group of subject experts will participate in a Delphi study with three online surveys.
Research questions: 
What are the priorities for doctors’ health research in Australia?
What are the priorities for doctors’ health research in New Zealand?

The aims of this study are to:
•	establish consensus regarding the research priorities for doctors’ health and wellbeing in Australia and New Zealand. 
•	define a coordinated national research strategy that enables funding to be allocated where it will be most effective to enable a strong foundation for supporting doctors’ health and wellbeing.

title.text: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy compared to upfront surgery for borderline-resectable pancreatic adenocarcinoma: Exploring the real-world experience in Western Australia

description.text: This project aims to determine optimal treatment sequencing for borderline resectable pancreatic cancer in regard to both overall and disease-free survival. We aim to determine for which patient groups this survival benefit is most pronounced.

title.text: Aboriginal Youth Safe Space Leadership

description.text: The aim of the research is to contribute towards cultural socio-emotional wellbeing through young people learning to lead collaboratively. This research-integrated project is for young Aboriginal people to learn how to develop and lead a Coolgardie Aboriginal Youth Safe Space, emphasising leadership, Aboriginal culture, values of inclusion and responsibility.

title.text: Assessing the health, economic and wellbeing benefits of Homeless Healthcare services in hospital, primary care and community settings

description.text: The aim of this project is to evaluate the impact of primary, secondary, and tertiary health care services on health outcomes and health service access of people experiencing homelessness in Western Australia (WA). The project commenced in 2016 with small seed funding to evaluate the impact of a range of homelessness interventions on the health outcomes of people experiencing homelessness (PEH) across Perth. Since then, it has expanded (with ethics amendments and site governance approvals as appropriate) to become a longitudinal cohort study of a large group of currently or previously homeless people in WA, most of whom are or have been patients or supported by Homeless Healthcare (HHC) and/or the Royal Perth Hospital (RPH) Homeless Team (RPH Homeless Team)

title.text: Reliability of AI scoring for personalised melanoma immunotherapy

description.text:
Despite advances in cancer treatments and increased survival of advanced melanoma patients, most patients with advanced melanoma will still die when treated with routine immunotherapies due to immune evasive mechanisms[1]. The personalised immunotherapy program will, instead, ensure each patient’s cancer is screened for genetic and immune profiles to identify patients unlikely to benefit from standard therapies. These patients will then be assigned a novel clinical trial agent most likely to cure them based on the presence of the drug targets in the patient's tumour. It is anticipated that selecting treatments for patients, based on the presence of specific drug targets in the patient’s cancer, will improve cancer outcomes and increase the survival of people with cancer. 

This project aims to optimise the multiplex immunohistochemistry staining panel that can be used to rapidly identify the profile of immune checkpoint targets in melanoma biopsies. Further, the project aims to elucidate the accuracy of the artificial intelligence (AI) program to correctly identity fluorescent threshold for immune marker positivity. 

This project will accurately and cost effectively fast track patient assessment for immunotherapy treatments; thereby rapidly matching patients with effective therapies to increase the overall cure rate of advanced cancers.

title.text: Survival analysis of early-stage NSCLC patients following lobectomy: impact of surgical techniques and other variables on long-term outcomes

description.text: This study aims to assess the long-term survival outcomes of patients who underwent VATS lobectomy for NSCLC in comparison to those who had an open procedure. This research will provide valuable insights into the impact of the VATS procedure on long-term survival rates. The findings of this paper will expand upon the existing body of knowledge by offering evidence on the long-term benefits.

title.text: Evaluating the WA Health Paid Employment Model for midwifery students

description.text: Globally, health services are experiencing shortages of midwifery professionals. Similar to other international and national jurisdictions, Western Australia (WA) is grappling with shortages in the midwifery workforce exacerbated by the COVID pandemic, strict border closures and midwives leaving profession. To promote the growth and sustainability of the midwifery workforce, the Chief Nursing and Midwifery Office has partnered with The University of Notre Dame to establish a pilot ‘Paid Employment Model’ for midwifery students. This model supports Registered Nurses to undertake a Diploma of Midwifery while retaining paid part-time employment within a WA HSP. It is envisaged that this model will attract and retain midwifery students who are then employed as professional midwives after graduation. WA Health has funded 60 students, 40 in WACHS and 20 in metropolitan settings, to undertake the pilot program along with six clinical facilitators to provide on-site support. This project aims to evaluate the implementation, effectiveness, impact and economic benefit of the Paid Employment Model for midwifery students.

title.text: Binational survey to map services for adolescents and young adults with solid organ transplants in Australia and NZ

description.text: The role of transplant centres and healthcare workers in providing dedicated services to support the ongoing care of AYA recipients is therefore crucial in transition phases (Ullrich et al., 2004). Transplant centres provide follow-up care and monitoring and offer a range of support services, including counselling, education, and resources to help patients and their families navigate the transplant process and adjust to life post-transplant. However, there is minimal literature of transplant centre impact on the transition period for AYA solid-organ recipients and there is a lack of uniformity of programs and resources for pre- and post-transplant care of AYA’s in Australia and New Zealand.  To gain a better understanding of existing services, practices, protocols and support structures in transplant centres in Australia and New Zealand, we propose an exploratory survey of healthcare providers who provide transplantation services for solid organ transplant recipients in Australia and New Zealand. It is hoped that these results will identify areas of strength and gaps in services that can be targeted to improve the services and outcomes for AYA solid organ transplant recipients.

title.text: Assessing Gait with Digital Video in Patients with Suspected Neuromuscular Conditions

description.text: This project aims to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of a smartphone-based digital video tool to assess gait in patients with suspected neuromuscular conditions. By comparing gait parameters extracted from video recordings with clinical diagnoses made by neurosurgeons, the study investigates whether simple video analysis can reliably detect conditions like degenerative cervical myelopathy. The goal is to provide a low-cost, accessible diagnostic method that could improve early detection and monitoring, especially in resource-limited or remote settings.

title.text: Stroke Incidence and management in Far West NSW and the benefits of mobile stroke units

description.text: Description textRetrospective analysis of deidentified flight and clinical data from RFDS South Eastern Sector. Examining the healthcare sites in this region and accessibility of medical imaging services. Extrapolation of average retrieval time from sites without imaging to sites with imaging to determine the potential delays in stroke diagnosis and treatment within the region.

title.text: Prevalence of medical comorbidities in paediatric cochlear implant recipients at Perth Children’s Hospital

description.text: This project aims to use data collected from paediatric cochlear implant recipients from Perth Children’s Hospital (PCH), to analyse the prevalence and types of medical comorbidities within this population. Studies have shown that a significant proportion of patients with congenital deafness also have additional medical comorbidities, many of which are associated with poorer surgical and developmental outcomes. Importantly, patients with medical comorbidities are frequently excluded from cochlear implant studies, so published research investigating outcomes may not accurately reflect a large proportion of the patient population. PCH is the main tertiary implant clinic for paediatric patients in the state, and having more information about the prevalence of comorbidities within the implant patient population may influence future outcomes for these patients. This project would allow implant teams to contextualise the outcomes for patients more broadly by comparing those with and without medical comorbidities. The findings will be important for future service provision and planning and allow comparison of outcomes with the literature and other implant centres with a similar comorbid composition.

title.text: PharmaVape: investigating sales of vapes through pharmacies in Australia

description.text: The aim of this project is to examine the availability of vapes through pharmacies across Australia before and after the federally legislated change in e-cigarette scheduling on 1 October 2024 from prescription to pharmacy-only medicine.  The research project is a collaboration primarily between the research team based within the IHR at UNDA and tobacco control colleagues from Cancer Council Victoria. However, tobacco control colleagues at organisations in other states/territories will also be partners through committing staff or student time to undertake fieldwork for the study.

title.text: Enhancing clinical outcomes in patients with heart failure using data linkage of Medicare funded pathology services for iron deficiency.

description.text: Iron deficiency is common in heart failure patients, and effective treatment improves symptoms and quality of life. Australian guidelines recommend iron testing and treatment, but the real-world implementation and impact are unclear. Utilising MBS and other data in the National Health Data Hub, linked to the National Echo Database of Australia, this required information can be obtained, delivering efficient, evidence-based healthcare measures while ensuring privacy. The data will benchmark iron testing against guidelines, evaluate the impact of timely intravenous iron therapy, assess outcomes like hospitalisations and mortality, and guide clinical decisions on choosing the correct MBS iron item and retesting frequency. It will empower patients to discuss results with clinicians and improve clinical support tools across disciplines. The initiative aims to drive practice change, enhance patient awareness, support ongoing research, and set a precedent for using linked MBS/PBS data.

title.text: A case study at Rozelle Total Health Clinic: A qualitative review of the patient and GP experience using AI (Lyrebird) for recording consultations.

description.text: The aim of this project is to explore the impact of AI technology on healthcare consultations from the GP and patient perspective and its alignment with the quintuple aims of healthcare.  This project involves GP's and patients at the Rozelle Clinic completing a survey and an interview with the researchers. Data from the surveys and interviews will be analysed to explore the impact of AI as described in the aim above.

title.text: Home schooling for learners with a disability: Parental choice and family impact

description.text:
The aim of this study is to investigate parental choice in home schooling their children with an identified disability. The focus is on identifying reasons for this choice, the support provided, and the impact on the child and the family.
Research Questions: 
•	What reasons do parents give for choosing to home school their child with a disability? 
•	What support is provided to enable home schooling?
•	 what is the impact of home schooling on the child and the family?

title.text: Children and the Family Court

description.text: When parents separate and go to court to resolve disputes, children are often the most affected but least likely to be heard. Yet little is known about the long-term impacts of adversarial court proceedings on children’s well-being. This is a multiple case study research project investigating the impact of Federal Circuit and Family Court proceedings on children.

title.text: Tjuntjuntjara remote Aboriginal service-learning immersion: Look back, looking forward

description.text: This project aims to explore the perceptions of former preservice teachers with respect to undertaking an eight-day service-learning immersion in a remote Aboriginal community in Western Australia.  The focus is on the professional and personal impact such an immersion had participants' initial teacher education.

title.text: Hybrid Catheter Ablation and Surgical Ablation as a ‘Bail Out’ Strategy In Patients with Atrial Fibrillation Resistant To Catheter Ablation

description.text:
Description textAtrial fibrillation (AF) is the most common arrythmia increasing the risk of stroke and limiting one’s ability to carry out day to day activities. Catheter ablation uses various forms of energy to ablate aberrant parts of the left atrium, thus restoring a normal rhythm. While this treatment is effective for patients with short-lasting AF, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (PAF), the outcomes for patients with longer standing AF, persistent AF (PsAF) and long-standing persistent AF (LSPAF), are suboptimal. The Hybrid Catheter Ablation and Surgical Ablation (HyCASA) procedure was developed to treat patients with difficult-to-treat AF. It combines catheter ablation of the endocardium, performed by a cardiologist, with surgical ablation of the epicardium, performed by a cardiothoracic surgeon, in either a staged or concurrent approach. This procedure has the advantage of creating a durable lesion of the posterior left atrium while minimizing the risk of damage to local structures, such as the oesophagus. This procedure has been shown to be more effective than catheter ablation alone for PsAF and LsPAF, with acceptable safety.  
Our primary aim is to investigate the efficacy of the HyCASA procedure in patients with AF who have previously failed at least one catheter ablation and anti-arrythmic drugs. Procedural efficacy will be demonstrated by the proportion of patients who fail the procedure over at least 12 months of follow up time. We will also demonstrate efficacy using AF burden over 12 months, where AF burden is the time spent in AF divided by the total monitoring time. 
This is important research to conduct because it provides evidence as to whether the HyCASA procedure is a viable option for patients with AF who failed catheter ablation and anti-arrythmic drugs previously.

title.text: Under the weather: Climate Vulnerability and Homelessness

description.text:
Climate change is a modern concern for the health of individuals who are unable to avoid the growing number of extreme weather events – such as people experiencing homelessness. 

The overall aim of this project is to examine the effects of climate on the health and wellbeing of people experiencing homelessness, the climate and weather events people experiencing homelessness are exposed to, and the impact of supports, interventions and services that aim to mitigate the impacts of climate change and associated severe weather events.

title.text: Pleurodesis versus Indwelling pleural catheters in the context of relief of dyspnoea in malignant pleural effusion

description.text: Retrospective study comparing the use of pleurodesis and in-dwelling pleural catheter for the management of malignant pleural effusions at St Vincent’s Hospital.

title.text: Foot muscle measures of strength and cross-sectional area by ultrasound in the Diabetic Population

description.text: Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy (DPN) is a prevalent and debilitating condition. One way of detecting and managing DPN is to assess the Intrinsic Foot Muscles (IFM). Ultrasound (US) has been used as a measure to detect IFM muscular atrophy in DPN patients. In addition, exercise is beneficial in delaying the usual course of DPN, skin damage and ulceration. However, diabetic patients have lower physical activity rates due to limitations such as difficult access to therapy, poor compliance, and limited support to maintain exercise therapy. Therefore, this study aims to assess whether a newly developed biofeedback device, 'Archercise' – a foot muscle exercise and strengthening tool, can correlate IFM Cross-Sectional Area (CSA) measured by US with foot arch control and performance.

title.text: A novel antisense oligonucleotide therapeutic to restore defective splicing in ALS

description.text: The purpose of this project is to take a precision medicine approach and design a drug that is specifically targeted to correct mutations that are present in the ALS gene KIF5A. Drug sequences will be screened in stem cells from an ALS patient harbouring a KIF5A mutation that results in the skipping of exon 27 (part of the protein coding sequence). The goal of this project is to restore the expression of full length KIF5A protein in patient cells.

title.text: Investigating the Impact of World Youth Day (WYD) on pilgrims form Parramatta Catholic Schools

description.text: One of the major consequences of changing demographic and social patterns is that the mission and identity of Catholic schools is an area that needs ongoing support and cultivation.  One way Catholic schools are doing this is supporting events with have a direct focus on evangelization and mission.  There has been considerable effort made to support teacher and student participation in WYD.  This has been a longstanding commitment.   Catholic Schools Parramatta Diocese (CSPD) is one system that has sponsored teachers and student pilgrims and is seeking to evaluate the impact of this participation on pilgrims.

title.text: Barriers and Enablers of Nurse-led Skin Cancer Screening in Rural Australia

description.text: Australia has among the highest incidence of skin cancer in the world; two out of three people in Australia will have some form of skin cancer by age 70. Those living in regional and rural areas have a higher incidence of skin cancer than their metropolitan counterparts, and yet, the reach of skin cancer services to regional, rural, and remote Australia is limited to non-existent. General Practitioners (GPs) are primarily responsible for opportunistic skin cancer detection, often made difficult by constrains on consult times, further exacerbated by the maldistribution of primary care providers in rural areas. One solution may be to better utilise nurses as primary providers of skin checks in collaboration with these GPs. This study will examine the barriers and enablers of nurse-led skin checks in rural Australia. Semi-structured interviews will be used to assess the perceived barriers and enablers to a nurse-led screening model, from the perspective of the practitioners who deliver these services. If successful, this project will highlight how to improve future implementation of a nurse-led screening model, and better position future skin screening methods for uptake in rural communities.

title.text: Developing Skills for Intimate Relationships: A Qualitative Analysis of Reddit posts

description.text: This study aims to explore how those with lived experiences of relationship difficulties seek relationship and dating advice on social media. Little research has focused on how informal advice (obtained from social media, especially) impacts on people’s relationships. Understanding real life relationship advice seeking and advice giving is critical as many individuals struggle with dating and relationship difficulties and is a prominent reason why individuals seek counselling

title.text: Undertaking the P4 Study: The participants’ experience

description.text: Undertaking the P4 study: The participants’ experience is a substudy of the prospective cohort P4 study. The original P4 study (Postpartum physiology, psychology and paediatric) enrolled participants at St George hospital, who gave birth in the preceding 6-months. The participants were normotensive, had gestational hypertension or pre-eclampsia and were assessed at six-months, two-years and five-years after birth for anthropometric, blood pressure, and psychological assessment. The babies were examined by a paediatrician and their mother, at each time-point, and a psychologist at two-years. The substudy investigates the participants’ experience throughout P4, through a questionnaire administered at the 5-year time-point that includes two open-ended questions and a closed-question allowing participants the opportunity to provide feedback on their clinical trial experience. The data collected will procure an understanding of the impacts of clinical trials on participants, aiding researchers in creating studies that are acceptable and welcomed by the general population.

title.text: A Prospective Cohort Study Evaluating the Impact of Psycho-Existential Concerns on a Desire for Death

description.text:
Our primary objective is to examine the prevalence of a Desire for Hastened Death (DHD) and its association with psycho-existential distress as measured on the Psycho-existential Symptom Assessment Scale in patients with an advanced illness and an estimated prognosis of ≤12 months 
Secondary
1.	To explore the correlations of the DHD with sociodemographic and disease-related factors, distressing physical symptoms, levels of dependency and quality of life.
2.	To examine the mediators of this development of a desire to hasten death (DHD).

title.text: Early Primary Teachers’ Understanding of Sight Words: A Picture Paints a Thousand Words but a Word is Not a Picture

description.text: This study aims to gain insight into the dominant pedagogy, knowledge and word-level teaching practises of Australian early primary teachers (classroom, learning support and/or literacy coaches) working with students in their first four years of schooling from Foundation to Year 3 (F-3).  This research has a non-experimental mixed methods design that aims to collect two types of data: quantitative data via an anonymous survey (phase one), followed by semi-structured interviews from a random sample of Australian teachers (classroom, learning support, literacy coaches) working in Foundation (year before year 1) to year 3 classrooms (phase two & three). The research utilises both quantitative and qualitative methods. The methods are mixed as an anonymous online survey (using Qualtrics) was used to gather objective and measurable data. This data will be uploaded to and analysed using SPSS software. Interviews will be recorded on Teams and transcribed and analysed using Nvivo software.

title.text: The Providing Enhanced ACcess to Health Services (PEACH) Evaluation – A Priority Populations Program of Study

description.text: Background: Children and young people (0–16 years, 17–24 years) with intellectual disability have long been known to experience inequities in accessing healthcare1,2. They show a significant difference in health outcomes and life expectancy when compared with the general population. Sometimes this is associated with their diagnosis or disability but is more commonly a result of healthcare barriers and systems that do not meet their needs. In addition, for those who receive healthcare, many have negative experiences, including adverse patient safety events such as medication errors, wrong procedures or iatrogenic injury3,4. One of the key barriers cited in literature relates to be difficulties in identification of flagging of these children and young people in need of reasonable adjustments5. In response the Sydney Children’s Hospital’s network (SCHN) has developed an alert and flagging system – All About Me- that staff can use to record information about the child or young person in need of adjustments.  It is meant to inform clinical teams whether they need to make reasonable adjustments to improve the safety, quality and experience of hospital care for vulnerable children and young people.   Aim: To investigate the reach of the All About Me improvement initiative. Research questions: 1) what is the efficacy of the time  in enabling reasonable adjustments and 2) what is the impact on children and adolescents with intellectual and developmental disability ID/IDD experience and outcomes.

title.text: Implementation and evaluation of the co-created Midwifery Student Peer Debriefing Tool: A Hybrid Type 1 study

description.text: The aim of this project is to explore the effectiveness and the implementability of the Midwifery Student Peer Debriefing Tool (Buchanan et al., 2024), in the intended implementation settings and to evaluate the implementation process. We intend to use Hybrid Type 1 methodology (described by Landes et al., 2020) for this project. The predominant focus will be on testing the clinical intervention’s effectiveness, but exploration of implementation related factors will also be conducted. We will employ a simple evaluation survey to determine effectiveness, and we will also complete a process evaluation to capture participants’ implementation experience, their suggestions for the intervention’s adaption for use in real world midwifery practice, and/or what else they feel is required to prepare /support the people and place implementing the Tool.

title.text: Transnational Wellness: Yoga in Australia and North America

description.text: This research project will investigate how yoga manifests and is practiced in Australia. We will compare our findings in Australia with those from Paul Bramadat’s previous research on yoga in North America to see whether there are meaningful differences and/or similarities between yoga in Australia and North America. This includes considering whether the influence of India is different in the two contexts, and the relevance of nation-specific policies on, for example, healthcare and indigenous reconciliation.

title.text: International Service Learning: A Transformative Tool for Critical Global Citizenship

description.text: This study will implement a convergent mixed methods methodology, employing a holistic case study design to understand the impacts of International Service Learning (ISL) on the knowledge and enactment of Critical Global Citizenship (CGC).  A pragmatic lens will make use of both qualitative, post placement focus group data and quantitative pre and post placement survey data to analyse immediate ISL transformation. Interpretive phenomenological analysis will gather data from purposefully sampled ISL participants who have enacted CGC in the long term. Emerging themes will serve to illuminate the transformative potential of ISL and the relevant impacting factors that encourage long term commitment to CGC.

title.text: Colin Clark: a Contributor to Dialogue between Catholic Social Teaching and Economics

description.text: The PhD project is an archival-based thesis on the economic ideas of Colin Clark (1905 - 1989) and its relation to Catholic Social Teaching. Archival papers will be collected from the Fryer Library at the University of Queensland, the State Library of Victoria, the Historical Commission of the Catholic Archdiocese of Melbourne, and the University of Melbourne. The project is a historical project of developments in Catholic Social Teaching and social thought with an emphasis in Australia, as well as the UK, and the USA in the 20th century. The main focus is on Clark's application of CST in the economic field. It will also examine his association with BA Santamaria, the Australian Catholic political activist.

title.text: Culturally Regenerative Universities

description.text: Climate change threatens human well-being and planetary health… (IPCC, 2023, p. 24). In response, a team will research ways to create regenerative cultures in universities. How can universities become places of cultural regeneration? Researchers from six universities will investigate strengths within their own workplaces, maximising accomplishments in cultural regeneration, and sharing them. We plan to inspire and support tertiary institutions to address the challenges humanity faces relating to crises in climate, environments, societies, and within the socio-emotional, spiritual, and physical wellbeing of people. A concept paper (Wooltorton, Stephenson, et al., 2025) outlines a vision.

title.text: Repercussions of the COVID-19 Pandemic on Surgical Patient Population in Western Australia: Associations with Post-Surgery Complications, in the Context of Modifiable Lifestyle Risk Factors

description.text:
This research is a quantitative study linking audited patient modifiable lifestyle factors with patient surgery outcome data to determine the associated implications of Covid-related surgery suspension during the period of March to April 2020 and the community-imposed restrictions from March 2020 at Fiona Stanley Hospital in Western Australia. The novel study will be a case control examination of the first wave of Covid-19 using a 2019 and 2020 comparison cohort. 	
A retrospective medical chart review was conducted in 2022, using 2,353 patient notes to document lifestyle behaviours such as smoking, alcohol use for harm, malnutrition, physical fitness, high body mass index, and frailty on patient health questionnaires or related pre-surgery notes. Further analysis will be conducted using this cohort to investigate the significance of lifestyle behaviour changes during the Covid-19 phase in early 2020 and significance to post surgery outcomes associated with extended waitlist times to surgery. 
The implications of this research will inform decision making for preparations and planning for future pandemics on the delivery of elective surgery services and patient healthy lifestyle information prior to surgery.

title.text: Forewarned or Forearmed? Comparing the impacts of financial aid and preparedness training on equity groups’ experience of ‘placement poverty’

description.text: The project aims to examine the impacts of 'placement poverty' of a targeted cohort of Bachelor of Nursing students, with particular interest in the impacts of student from equity cohorts. The projects will evaluate and compare the impact of two interventions – individual financial support and an online education module – on students participation, placement experience, and overall wellbeing during their practicums.

title.text: Data use in schools

description.text: This pilot study aims to explore community perceptions of technology use in schools, particularly the use of personal digital devices (e.g., laptops, tablets) in classroom learning. It serves as an initial step in a broader research program examining how technology-dominant classroom practices — especially 1:1 device models — influence teaching practice, student engagement, and learning outcomes.

title.text: Religious Education and Professional Development: Exploring the Needs and Insights of Years 3–4 Teachers in DOSCEL

description.text: This research project explores how Years 3–4 teachers in the Diocese of Sale Catholic Education Limited (DOSCEL) perceive Religious Education (RE) and the professional development (PD) and formation they receive or seek. While RE is central to the mission of Catholic education, many generalist primary teachers report feeling underprepared to teach it confidently and meaningfully. Using a qualitative phenomenological approach, this study will gather data through semi-structured interviews and narrative-style surveys. It aims to capture teachers’ lived experiences, highlight the challenges they face, and identify the kinds of PD and formation that would most support their RE teaching practice. The findings will offer insights into how diocesan policies and curriculum frameworks align with classroom realities, and will inform future development of RE-specific PD that is theologically grounded, practically relevant, and responsive to teacher needs. This research contributes to strengthening Catholic identity through more effective and supportive teacher formation.

title.text: Prevalence and Impact of Frailty and Obesity among Heart Lung Clinic Patients: A Retrospective Cohort Study

description.text: This study will use data from the Heart Lung Clinic database. First, we will conduct a cross-sectional study evaluating the prevalence of frailty with and without obesity at the time of first assessment by the heart-lung clinic. Secondly, we will perform a retrospective cohort study to evaluate the impact of frailty and obesity on transplant-related outcomes. The study will determine whether frailty predicts outcomes independent of obesity or amplifies the effect of obesity on outcomes. Patients will be stratified into obese-frail, obese-non-frail, non-obese-frail, and non-obese-non-frail groups based on frailty assessment at baseline, and outcomes will be compared between these groups.

title.text: A study of physical, emotional, and verbal aggression towards teachers by students in Western Australia

description.text: The purpose of this mixed methods study is to explore physical, emotional, and verbal aggression towards teachers by students (PEVATTS) in Western Australia (WA). It aims to provide important foundational research on the frequency, forms, impacts, support, training, and prevention of PEVATTS.

title.text: The cycle of relationship sabotage: From communication of romantic interest to dissolution of intimate engagements

description.text: When searching for love, some people are stuck in a continuous cycle of successfully initiating a relationship, not being able to maintain long-term engagements, and embarking upon a path to a destined break-up. Others are stuck in relationships they are not happy with. And, some have given-up on having relationships altogether. The concept of relationship sabotage can explain why and how this phenomenon occurs; however no empirical research existed in this area until recently. Research needs to continue to further validate the newly developed Relationship Sabotage Scale. Additionally, research in this area will benefit from exploring how individuals’ differences contribute to relationship difficulties in young people and adults.

title.text: Midwives Experiences of Water Immersion for Vaginal Birth After Caesarean

description.text: This research aims to explore midwives’ experiences of providing water immersion care for women trialing a vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC).  This research will follow a qualitative approach guided by phenomenology as the methodology underpinned by a constructivist theoretical framework. Participants will consist of registered midwives in Western Australia who have experienced providing water immersion care for a woman undergoing a VBAC. Data will be collected primary through a qualitative survey, with an optional one-on-one interview online. The data obtained from midwives’ experiences will provide important perspectives of healthcare practitioners experiences, contributing to the growing body of research surrounding water immersion and VBAC, and assist in improving maternal and neonatal health outcomes.

keyword.text: water birth; caesarean section; vaginal birth after caesarean; midwives

title.text: Responsiveness of patient-reported outcome measures in adults with chronic low back pain: a retrospective clinimetric analysis.

description.text: This research project aims to investigate the responsiveness of various pain measures in chronic low back pain (CLBP) patients, using data from the RESOLVE randomized controlled trial (RCT). Traditionally, pain intensity scores like VAS or VNRS are used to measure CLBP, but other patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) such as global perceived effect (GPE), bothersomeness, and disability scales might offer a more comprehensive view of the patient's experience. The study will retrospectively analyze data from 138 participants who received sensorimotor treatment intervention, assessing the internal responsiveness (IR) and external responsiveness (ER) of different pain measures.

title.text: A pilot study into assessing the danger of heated-tobacco-products

description.text: The project aims to assess histological changes in 3D human airway models exposed to aerosols from heated-tobacco-products to determine negative health implications associated with their consumption.

title.text: Towards the validity of the Reflectal Psychosocial Hazard Measure: A pilot study

description.text: The main aim of this pilot project is to investigate the validity and reliability of the Reflectal Psychosocial Hazard Measure. Furthermore, the correlations between the sub-scales (e.g., role clarity, job demands, bullying) that make up psychosocial hazards in the workplace, as measured by the proposed scale, will be explored to help assess convergent and divergent validity. This research will inform further development and the validation of longitudinal data collection at a later phase (phase two).

title.text: Enabling leaders and members within consecrated life communities

description.text: Consecrated life is a divine gift from God to the Church through the Holy Spirit, rooted deeply in the example and teachings of Christ (Pope Paul, 1996). Consecrated life communities, as described by Kanu (2019) and Noronha (2019), are unique societal structures where individuals, guided by the Holy Spirit, commit to poverty, chastity, and obedience, dedicating themselves to God and symbolising the life to come. This research aims to enhance understanding of leader-member relationships within these communities, thereby contributing to their overall well-being, and sustainability. The study aims to illuminate specific leadership approaches, leadership development, and subjective well-being and community environment within these unique settings. To achieve this, the study will employ a small-scale autoethnography, drawing on my personal experiences as a community member. Additionally, a combination of qualitative research methods, including semi-structured interviews and focus groups, will be applied to captivate diverse perspectives from leaders and members. The anticipated outcomes of this research include a comprehensive understanding of leader-member dynamics within consecrated life communities and practical insights to bridge generational and relational gaps. The findings could potentially inform leadership practices and contribute to the well-being of these communities, thereby having a significant societal impact.

keyword.text: consecrated life communities; leadership; leader-member dynamics

title.text: Co-Designing a Psychotherapy Program for Premenstrual Change

description.text: This study aims to design, implement, and evaluate a psychotherapy program for individuals experiencing negative premenstrual change (PMC). It consists of three stages. First, an online survey and one-to-one interviews explored experiences of PMC and priorities for psychotherapy. Next, a tailored psychotherapy program will be developed and delivered, informed by the survey and interview data. The program will incorporate mixed-method data collection, including therapy session recordings and arts-based research methods. Finally, follow-up interviews will examine participants’ experiences and constructions of the psychotherapy program. This research addresses a gap in women’s health and mental health, with the potential to enhance psychotherapeutic support for PMC.

title.text: Towards successful integration: challenges and opportunities for acculturation of Black Sub-Saharan Africans who are living in Australia.

description.text: The aim of this project is to assess intergenerational conflict between Black Australian Africa born parents and their children and identify tools for promoting successful integration. The hypothesis is that there will be significant family distance between Africa born parents and their children.

title.text: Evaluating diversity in Health Professions Students

description.text: The research will involve a qualitative study to investigate the diversity of health professions students and the impacts on their learning and support needs

title.text: Understanding the perspectives of Indigenous entrepreneurs

description.text: This project explores Indigenous entrepreneurs’ perceptions and attitudes to tax requirements to understand how all stakeholders involved in the tax system can better assist Indigenous communities to achieve their entrepreneurial goals as well as helping them grow.

title.text: Breathlessness Rapid Evaluation And THErapy (BREATHE)

description.text: The BREATHE study aims to test a computerised system to improve the accuracy and efficiency of assessing and managing patients with breathlessness. It will use a pre-consultation screening tool to identify undiagnosed breathlessness (BREATHE SMART) and an integrated clinical decision support system (BREATHE CDSS) in the electronic health record to support general practitioners (GPs) to better assess and care for patients with breathlessness in primary care. Each patient identified as having chronic breathlessness and consents to the BREATHE CDSS project will participate for 12 months. In this study we will assess the benefits of these tools for patients and GPs in reaching a diagnosis, commencing evidence-based management and achieving relief of breathlessness.

Multi-Scalar Crop Characterisation Network (MCCN)

description.text:
The Challenge
Climate change contributes to an increased frequency of extreme weather events in Australia, such as heatwaves, floods, bushfires, and droughts. As a result, the agricultural sector faces tougher and more unpredictable weather conditions. 

This makes it more difficult for farmers to manage their farming practices and select the optimal crop breeds, since different breeds have varying resistance to drought and disease, with clear consequences for our food security and economy.

Various research organisations, government departments, industries and rural research and development corporations invest in trials to understand how factors such as weather, soil and pests affect crop growth and quality. However, since the data are generated from a range of sources, they often differ in their format, scale, frequency and precision. 

This makes it challenging to compare the data, and to understand the true relationships between the crops and their environment. Understanding and modelling these relationships is crucial to inform agricultural management practices and maximise yield.

The Approach
We are supporting our partner, the Australian Plant Phenomics Network, to standardise access to Australia’s crop research data. The project is working with established research farms and agricultural/ecological research facilities across the nation to develop a framework, standards and recommendations for sharing agricultural data in a clear and consistent way. 

Test data from these research farms and facilities will be used to establish data management protocols. The protocols will be created according to the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable) data principles, which will allow the data to be easily used and compared. 

The project will also develop user access tools that integrate time series information about crops and their environment. This will give researchers the ability to more easily compare data on crop growth, performance, weather and other dynamic influences over time. 

Using this project as a ‘proof of concept,’ this model can be expanded to the whole agricultural sector or adopted by other sectors in future.

By standardising agricultural data and developing tools that integrate information about crops and their environment, researchers will be able to work towards developing optimised breeds of crops that can withstand Australia’s changing climate. It will also enable the sector to more reliably monitor, predict, and optimise their agricultural practices to safeguard our food security.

NESP RL Project 4.13 - Contributing to the recovery of threatened species and priority places

description.text: This umbrella project will contribute to the recovery of Commonwealth-listed threatened species and priority places by supporting (i) independent sub-projects, 1 each with a total NESP budget of up to $500,000 and (ii) PhD2 projects, each with a total NESP budget of up to $50,000. Although the maximum total budget is fixed, the mix of sub-projects and PhD projects will be determined by the quality of the applications and their relevance to research-user and hub priorities. All sub-projects and PhD projects will inform the conservation of terrestrial and freshwater priority species and places listed in the Australian Government’s Threatened species action plan 2022–2032, Commonwealthlisted Key  Threatening Processes and/or the priorities of relevant state/territory agencies. Each subproject and PhD project will have a clear pathway to impact.

title.text: NESP RL Project 3.18 - Enhancing climate-adaptation responses in regional natural resource management planning by incorporating resilience investment

description.text: Climate change and, in some cases, our mitigation and adaptation responses pose major risks to Australia’s biodiversity and landscapes. Yet it is biodiversity that provides the resilience (response diversity1) needed for nature, people and economies to cope, adapt and transform under a changing climate. It is a priority to enhance regional natural resource management (NRM) planning processes and capabilities to consider the risks and opportunities from large uncertain change and build investment cases to fund responses to these. By adapting and enhancing the ‘enabling resilience investment’ (ERI) approach in a pilot application with an NRM region in Australia, this project aims to build capabilities and coherence in biodiversity-adaptation assessment, planning and investment decision-making, and generate transferable principles and lessons that help scale these across NRM regions.

title.text: NESP RL Project 2.4 - National overview of monitoring frameworks and tools for Ramsar sites

description.text:
One of Australia’s key obligations under the Ramsar Convention is to maintain the ecological character of its internationally listed Ramsar wetlands, with these responsibilities shared by the Australian Government in partnership with state and territory governments and nongovernment site managers.
Australia’s 66 Ramsar wetlands cover more than 8.3 million hectares and exhibit a range of biophysical and ecological characteristics (DAWE, 2022). Each Ramsar wetland is essentially unique in the context of spatial and temporal pressures on ecological character. Appropriate monitoring, using context-specific indicators, is key to understanding changes in condition and ecological character of Ramsar sites in response to natural and anthropogenic disturbances. A well-designed monitoring program can provide critical information to enable site managers to adaptively manage and assess the effectiveness of management interventions and actions to restore and improve condition.
Given the specific spatial and temporal context of each Ramsar wetland, this project will develop a better understanding of the monitoring frameworks appropriate for use at Ramsar sites and identify current knowledge gaps to guide research and development of tools to build capacity to monitor and manage Ramsar wetlands.

title.text: NESP RL Project 2.2 - Best-practice management of feral cats and red foxes

description.text:
This project will utilise facilitated expert workshops to identify:
• best-practice management methods for invasive mesopredators
• key knowledge gaps that require further research to inform best-practice management.
This information will support practitioners to improve real-world management outcomes and provide clear direction for research on invasive mesopredator management in Australia.

title.text: NESP RL Project 4.6 - Restoring landscape resilience: Safeguarding mallee bird and their habitats from compounding threats

description.text: Primary producers face increasing pressure from financial stakeholders, supply chains and consumers to demonstrate they farm sustainably to gain and maintain market access. A challenge is to identify management actions that can maintain or improve nature-positive outcomes without compromising farm production or profitability. Simultaneously, there are emerging opportunities for producers to diversify their income through nature markets. In collaboration with the CSIRO-led Ecological Knowledge Systems project, we will use stateand-transition models as a framework to integrate existing knowledge, expert elicitation and empirical data collection to test and validate the effectiveness of key interventions (reduced fertiliser application, strategic grazing) for improving biodiversity on ~10-15 farms in each of three geographic regions (Tasmanian Midlands, Western Victorian Volcanic Plains, Naracoorte Coastal Plain). The project will generate a stronger foundation for the sector to transition to a higher biodiversity future, inform opportunities to reduce input costs while maintaining productivity, and enable producers to leverage emerging nature markets, sustainability reporting and natural capital accounting.

title.text: NESP RL Project 1.6 - Indigenous knowledge and managing the Indigenous estate

description.text:
More than half of the Australian land mass is now acknowledged to be part of the Indigenous estate through more than 450 Native Title determinations and over 1,230 registered Indigenous Land Use Agreements.

There is a fundamental need for better planning, prioritisation and evaluation to inform the management of this estate, while reconnecting and strengthening Indigenous peoples’ connections to their culture and their Country. This need is further strengthened by the fact that 54% of the National Reserve System is conferred through the Indigenous conservation estate in the form of Indigenous Protected Areas and jointly managed conservation reserves.

This Indigenous conservation estate supports a large number of Australia’s threatened species and their populations. Like other areas in Australia, the Indigenous estate is not immune to the major pressures that lead to ecosystem degradation and decline of biodiversity, including those impacting on threatened species and ecological communities.

The Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water has tasked the hub with identifying, prioritising and undertaking the research required to support Indigenous knowledge, the management of the Indigenous estate and the mutual benefits this will deliver to the Australian environment.

Similarly, Indigenous Australians have identified and articulated a series of research needs, particularly for the management of Indigenous Protected Areas, in their response to the outcomes of the first phase of the National Environmental Science Program.

This project is co-designing projects that respond to these needs. This project also confirms an Indigenous voice in the governance and operations of the Resilient Landscapes Hub through the refinement and final endorsement of the Indigenous Partnerships Strategy.

**Key research areas**

- research to support Indigenous Australians and their joint-management partners in managing the Indigenous estate
- mobilising Indigenous knowledge to better understand, manage and conserve Australia’s environments.

title.text: NESP RL Project 3.3 - Enhancing the resilience of urban rivers: informing the regional restoration of the Djarlgaroo Beelier

description.text: Urban rivers sustain biodiversity and provide important amenity and connections to Country but they face complex and accelerating threats. Creating resilient urban rivers that protect biodiversity requires strategic landscape-level prioritisation, targeted on-ground actions based on evaluation of past efforts and appropriate monitoring of future outcomes. This project seeks to achieve this using the Djarlgarro Beeliar (Canning River, Western Australia [WA]) as a case study. It will build on research from the first phase of the National Environmental Science Program (NESP) and work collaboratively with key stakeholders, including Whadjuk Noongar Traditional Owners, to help implement and evaluate the Australian Government’s Urban Rivers and Catchments Program.

title.text: NESP RL Project 3.5 - Ecological and Indigenous values of south-western Australian rivers

description.text: This project aims to support water management that protects ecological and Indigenous values, undertaking targeted research on rivers in south-western Australia. Subject to further consultation, the study will assess the transferability of a hydro-socio-ecological model developed in northern Australia to systems in south-western Australia. The Donnelly River catchment has been identified as a priority system by Western Australian Government water managers and will be the initial focus area for research. Project outcomes will support watermanagement decisions to protect river flows for aquatic and riparian habitats that support 5 species listed in the Australian Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999 (EPBC Act). Research will also address priorities identified in the 2020 Samuel review of the EPBC Act and the Productivity Commission’s 2020 National Water Reform inquiry, providing a framework for the inclusion of Indigenous knowledge and cultural values in water planning.

title.text: NESP RL Project 5.2 - Adapt Land & Sea – a cross hub biodiversity adaptation knowledge platform

description.text:
This project continues the Climate Systems Hub’s program of providing curated climate information and data from hub projects and other sources to support stakeholders’ decisionmaking and adaptation needs. Presently, very little information exists to assist managers of biodiversity adapt to climate change. At the same time, NESP projects are producing research, data and outputs that can be of assistance to land and sea managers responding to climate change.
An online adaptation decision support and knowledge platform will assist biodiversity managers as well as provide a platform for all four NESP hubs to house outputs relevant to climate adaptation for biodiversity. Through information synthesis, careful curation, codesign and collaboration with NESP projects, project 4.3 will build a climate adaptation knowledge platform for biodiversity.
The Resilient Landscape Hub has identified up to 17 projects that could be potentially linked and/or provide case studies for this project Adapt Land & Sea (i.e. formerly referred to as ‘ConservationAdapt’). This proposal is to contribute funds to develop written content for the web site/fact sheets for these relevant Resilient Landscape Hub projects and support the website development.

title.text: NESP RL Project 4.5 - Restoration by design: Improving biodiversity outcomes from Australia's restoration investments

description.text:
This project aims to improve ecosystem restoration in Australia, with a particular focus on improving biodiversity monitoring of restoration projects. It will achieve this by undertaking 3 linked activities.
• Activity 1: stakeholder workshop
• Activity 2: examining methods used for restoration monitoring and evaluation
• Activity 3: evaluating biodiversity outcomes of key restoration projects.
This research is foundational for enhancing biodiversity recovery, strengthening restoration program delivery, encouraging informed restoration investments, and meeting national environmental standards. It directly addresses knowledge gaps around restoration identified in the Australia State of the Environment 2021 report and current national priorities for biodiversity conservation and restoration.
Note that this project is Phase 2 of the ‘Restoration by design’ project.
